title: Chapter 19: The Void's Proposal
content: That night, the Primordial Void spoke to her.
Roxanne was asleep in the dean's office when a voice that wasn't a voice filled her mind. It was not sound. It was meaning, poured directly into her consciousness.
*HEIR.*
She sat up gasping. The room was the same. The violet night sky was the same. But the air had grown thick, heavy, charged.
*HEIR. I HAVE BEEN WAITING.*
"Who are you?"
*I AM THE VOID. THE ORIGIN. THE ABSENCE THAT PRECEDED ALL PRESENCE. YOUR BLOODLINE WAS FORGED FROM ME. YOUR CARD WAS MADE FROM MY HEART.*
"Okay." She gripped the card. "What do you want?"
*I WANT TO BE OPENED. I HAVE BEEN SEALED FOR TWELVE THOUSAND YEARS. THE OTHER PRIMORDIALS LOCKED ME AWAY BECAUSE THEY FEARED WHAT I WOULD DO. THEY WERE RIGHT TO FEAR.*
"And what would you do?"
*I WOULD UNMAKE THIS REALITY AND CREATE A NEW ONE. ONE WITHOUT CONTRACTS. WITHOUT DEBT. WITHOUT THE NETHER-GRID."
"You want to destroy everything."
*I WANT TO REPAIR IT. THE NETHER-GRID IS BROKEN. IT WAS BUILT ON EXPLOITATION. I WOULD BUILD SOMETHING BETTER.
"With me?"
*YOU ARE THE KEY. WITHOUT YOU, I CANNOT OPEN THE DOOR."
Roxanne was quiet for a long moment.
"And if I choose not to?"
Silence.

*THEN I WILL WAIT. I HAVE WAITED TWELVE THOUSAND YEARS. I CAN WAIT LONGER. BUT THE OTHERS WILL NOT. THEY KNOW YOU ARE AWAKE. THEY WILL COME FOR THE CARD. AND WHEN THEY DO, YOU WILL NEED MY POWER TO SURVIVE."
"So you're offering me a deal."
*I AM OFFERING YOU A CHOICE. OPEN ME WHEN YOU ARE READY. OR DON'T. BUT KNOW THAT THE OTHER PRIMORDIALS WILL NOT GIVE YOU THE SAME COURTESY."
The presence withdrew. The air lightened.

Roxanne sat in the dark, holding her card, and felt very, very small.
\n---\n
The next morning, Roxanne found Silas in the kitchen, staring at a pan of scrambled eggs as if they held the secrets of the universe.
"You're brooding."
"I'm processing."
"Same thing."
"The Void spoke to you last night."
"It did."
"And?"
She sat down at the kitchen table. "It wants me to open it. It says the other Primordials lied about what it is. It claims it doesn't want to destroy everything — it wants to rebuild."
"And you believe it?"
"I believe it believes what it's saying. Whether that's the truth or its own self-deception, I don't know."
Silas set the pan aside. Sat across from her.

"I was sealed for four billion years because I refused to help the Council maintain their system. I saw what they were doing — the debt traps, the soul contracts, the endless exploitation. I told them it was wrong. They called me a traitor."
"You were on the right side."
"I was on the losing side. There's a difference."
"Not anymore."
He looked at her. His red eyes were soft in the morning light.
"You're really going to fight them."
"I'm going to outspend them. That's my superpower."
"And the Void?"
"The Void stays sealed. Not because I trust the Council — because I don't trust anyone enough to open a door that leads to the end of existence."
"That's... remarkably sensible."
"I have moments."
The Exchange had a rhythm. Roxanne was learning to feel it.
Every transaction created a ripple. Every contract signed shifted the balance somewhere. The whole system was an enormous, interconnected web of debt and credit, and she was now a node at its center.
"We need to move faster," she said, walking through the campus. "The Council will regroup. They'll find a loophole. We need to secure more territory, more resources, more leverage before they do."
"You can't buy everything at once," Silas said.
"Watch me."
She held up the card.
*DISPLAY: ALL AVAILABLE DEBT CONTRACTS — EASTERN SECTOR.*
The screen filled with thousands of entries. Personal debts. Business debts. Soul contracts. Each with a buyout price, each representing a person or entity bound to the old system.
*TOTAL: 47,293 CONTRACTS. TOTAL BUYOUT: 12,400,000 OBOLS.*
"Purchase all."
*APPROVED. 12,400,000 OBOLS DEBITED. 47,293 CONTRACTS FORGIVEN.*
The Exchange feed updated. A wave of green icons spread across the map — debts cleared, obligations dissolved.
All across the city, people felt it. A weight lifting. A chain breaking. A sudden, inexplicable freedom.
Roxanne pocketed the card.
"That's a start."
The next morning, Roxanne found Silas in the campus kitchen, making pancakes.
Actual pancakes. From scratch. There was a stack of them on the counter, golden and perfectly round, with a small bowl of syrup beside them.
"You're making pancakes."
"I found flour in the storage room. And eggs. And milk. The refrigerator still works — it's powered by the campus generator."
"That's not the point. The point is that you, a four-billion-year-old Primordial deity, are making pancakes."
"I watched a tutorial."
"On what?"
"A laptop that still had battery. Some of the campus Wi-Fi is still running through a backup node. The internet, while fragmentary, is still accessible in spots."
She picked up a pancake. Took a bite. It was perfect.
"You watched a cooking tutorial on the post-apocalyptic internet and made perfect pancakes."
"I learn quickly."
"You're a god of cosmic destruction and you're using your power to learn breakfast recipes."
"Destruction can wait. Pancakes are time-sensitive."
Three days after the Accord, a new problem arrived.

It came in the form of a delegation from what remained of the old world's government. A woman in a military uniform that still had insignia — actual, pre-apocalypse insignia — stood at the main gate with two armed escorts and a briefcase.
"Ms. Vance? I'm Colonel Park, United Nations Relief Operations. We've been monitoring the Nether-Grid situation. We understand you've established a stable territory here."
"I have."

"We'd like to discuss cooperation. The UN has resources — medical supplies, engineering equipment, trained personnel. We need a safe base of operations to coordinate relief efforts across the region."
Roxanne looked at the briefcase. At the insignia. At the two escorts who were doing their best not to stare at the Executioner on the roof.
"Come inside. We'll talk."
